summary: O'Neil announces the first Data Skeptics Meetup featuring economist Suresh Naidu speaking on political uses and abuses of data. The talk will explore how data analytics used for consumer and voter prediction can also target dissidents, organize protests, and enable governments to 'push people around.' Naidu plans to contextualize modern data collection within a thousand-year history of government surveillance and examine its use in authoritarian regimes. The post notes the topic's timeliness given recent news coverage of such issues.
key_points: Data analytics tools for consumer behavior prediction have dual uses in political repression and resistance
Both authoritarian regimes and their opponents can leverage the same datasets and techniques
Government data collection has a thousand-year history that contextualizes current practices
The same technology enables targeting dissidents, organizing protests, and mobilizing leaders
money_quotes: The same datasets and analytics that let data scientists do prediction of consumer and voter behavior can also be used to forecast political opponents, mobilize likely leaders, solve collective problems and generally push people around.

From targeting dissidents and tax-evaders to organizing protests, the same datasets and analytics that let data scientists do prediction of consumer and voter behavior can also be used to forecast political opponents.
While a lot has been made of the use of technology for election campaigns, little discussion has focused on other political uses of data.
key_insight: The data science tools celebrated for marketing and campaigns are equally powerful for political surveillance and repression—a reality that deserves more scrutiny.
